title: MAP kinase activity is downregulated by phorbol ester during mouse oocyte maturation and egg activation in vitro
abstract:
MAP Kinase Activity Is Downregulated by Phorbol
Ester During Mouse Oocyte Maturation and Egg
Activation In Vitro
QING-YUAN SUN,1,2 SARA RUBINSTEIN,1 AND HAIM BREITBART1*
1Department of Life Sciences, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan, Israel
2Institute of Zoology, Academia Sinica, Beijing, China
ABSTRACT
The effects of protein kinase C
(PKC) stimulator, phorbol 12-myriatate 13-acetate (PMA),
on meiotic cell cycle regulation and mitogen-activated
protein (MAP) kinase changes have been studied in
mouse oocytes and eggs. The results showed that MAP
kinase activation itself was not necessary for germinal
vesicle breakdown (GVBD), but the ability of the oo-
plasm to phosphorylate MAP kinase was a prerequisite
for this event. At concentrations of 1.6 nM, PMA
effectively inhibited GVBD and MAP kinase activation,
suggesting that PMA inhibits GVBD by inhibiting mol-
ecule(s) upstream to MAP kinase. At concentrations of
16.2 nM, PMA induced metaphase-interphase transition
more effectively in eggs collected 19 hr after human
chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) administration than in
those collected 15 hr after hCG administration. The
degree of MAP kinase activity decrease was well
correlated with the time course and proportion of
pronuclear formation. On the other hand, when the
effect of PMA on cell cycle progression was abolished
by protein phosphatase inhibitor, okadaic acid, MAP
kinase was superactivated. The biologically inactive 4
a-phorbol 12,13-didecanoate (4 a-PDD) had no evident
effects on either GVBD and interphase transition or on
MAP kinase activity. Furthermore, the effects of PMA on
oocyte GVBD, egg activation, and MAP kinase activity
could be overcome by the speciﬁc PKC inhibitor,
calphostin C, suggesting the possible involvement of
this enzyme in the regulation of MAP kinase activity. The
results suggest that activation of PKC by PMA entrains
a cascade of events that ultimately inhibits MAP kinase
activation and GVBD in mouse oocytes and induces
MAP kinase inactivation and metaphase-interphase tran-
sition in mouse eggs. Mol. Reprod. Dev. 52:310–318,
1999.
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key Words: meiosis; cell cycle; protein kinase
INTRODUCTION
Mammalian oocytes are arrested at the prophase
(G2/M transition) of the ﬁrst meiotic division. In vitro,
fully-grown oocytes liberated from their follicles sponta-
neously reinitiate meiosis I, characterized by germinal
vesicle breakdown (GVBD), and proceed to metaphase
of the second meiotic division (M II), where they are
arrested again. Only after fertilization or artiﬁcial
activation will the eggs resume meiosis II, ﬁnish the
meiosis division, and ﬁnally form pronuclei. The ﬁrst
resumption of meiosis is different from the second in
that the former leads to interphase-metaphase transi-
tion of the cell cycle, while the latter is the opposite. The
cell cycle progression is regulated by cascade activation/
inactivation of protein kinases. One such protein kinase
is mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinase, which is
also termed extracellular-regulated kinase (ERK). This
is a serine/threonine kinase that requires dual phosphor-
ylation on threonine and tyrosine residues to become
fully activated (Nishida and Gotoh, 1993). The MAP
kinase activation occurs via a complex cascade of
kinases that include, immediately upstream, MAP ki-
nase kinase (MAPKK, MEK), which in turn is phos-
phorylated by one of the MAP kinase kinase kinases
(MAPKKK) (Gotoh and Nishida, 1995; Kasako et al.,
1996). Recent studies have shown that MAP kinase
activation, revealed either by using myelin basic pro-
tein (MBP) as a substrate or by observing its mobility
shift in the blot, is involved in the regulation of meiotic
cell cycle in mammalian oocytes (Verlhac et al., 1994;
Inoue et al., 1995; Dedieu et al., 1996; Fissore et al.,
1996). Some authors have shown that MAP kinase
activation is not implicated in the early events of
meiosis resumption, but rather in post-GVBD events
during mouse, rat, and goat oocyte maturation (So-
bajima et al., 1993; Verlhac et al., 1994; Dedieu et al.,
1996; Zernicka-Goetz et al., 1997). However, other lines
of evidence suggest that, as in Xenopus oocytes, MAP
kinase is actually involved in the induction of GVBD in
mouse, pig, and bovine oocytes (Chesnel and Eppig,
1995; Fissore et al., 1996; Inoue et al., 1998). Mitogen-
activated protein kinase has also been shown to be
involved in regulation of pronuclear envelope assembly/
disassembly after mouse egg fertilization (Moos et al.,
1995, 1996). Our recent work has shown that MAP
kinase inactivation is correlated with interphase transi-
Grant sponsor: Ihel Foundation.
*Correspondence to: Haim Breitbart, Department of Life Sciences,
Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan, 52900 Israel.
E-mail: breith@mail.biu.ac.il
Received 20 April 1998; Accepted 16 October 1998.
MOLECULAR REPRODUCTION AND DEVELOPMENT 52:310–318 (1999)
r 1999 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 tion after activation of mouse eggs by various chemical
stimuli such as A23187, ethanol, protein kinase inhibi-
tors, etc. (Sun et al., 1997).
Although the rise in intracellular Ca21 ([Ca21]i) ap-
pears to be sufficient to induce various events of egg
activation, its downstream molecules remain unknown.
The C-type protein kinase C (cPKC), which is generally
viewed as a calcium- and phospholipid-dependent en-
zyme, has gained much attention. Previous studies
have shown that activation of protein kinase C (PKC)
by pharmacological agents such as phorbol esters or
diacylglycerol inhibits GVBD in mouse oocytes (Urner
and Schorderet-Slatkine, 1984; Bornslaeger et al., 1986;
LeFevre et al., 1992). Protein kinase C activators have
also been reported to promote resumption of the cell
cycle and the formation of pronuclei or pronucleus-like
structures in mouse and hamster eggs (Colonna et al.,
1989; Gallicano et al., 1993; Moses and Kline, 1995).
Recently, it has been shown that PKC inhibitor abro-
gated the inactivation of maturation-promoting factor
(MPF) induced by A23187 (Colonna et al., 1997) and
that PKC activation is involved in the remodeling of a
mature egg into a zygote after fertilization (Gallicano et
al., 1997).All of these results suggest that PKC stimula-
tion keeps mouse oocytes and eggs arrested at the
interphase of the meiotic cell cycle. Since both PKC
modulation and MAP kinase cascade have been shown
to regulate meiotic cell cycle, and since MAP kinase
represents the convergence point for many signaling
pathways, we assume that the two may have cross-talk
in the signal transduction pathways during oocyte
maturation and egg activation. In the present study, we
have shown, for the ﬁrst time, that MAP kinase activity
is downregulated when PKC activator, phorbol 12-
myriatate 13-acetate (PMA), is used to inhibit GVBD in
oocytes and to stimulate metaphase-interphase transi-
tion in eggs.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Collection of Oocytes and Eggs
Germinal vesicle (GV)-stage oocytes were collected
from 4–6-week-old BALB/c mice by puncturing antral
follicles with a sharp needle in modiﬁed Tyrode’s (T6)
solution (Wood et al., 1987). Loosely bound cumulus
cells were removed by gentle pipetting through a
narrow-bore glass pipette. Germinal vesicle oocytes
from sexually premature (2-week-old) mice were also
collected as controls. Cumulus-enclosed metaphase II
(M II) eggs were recovered from the same strain by
treatment of the mice with PMSG and human chorionic
gonadotropin (hCG), 10 IU each, separated by a 48-hr
interval. Eggs were collected 15 or 19 hr after hCG
administration. Cumulus cells were removed by brief
exposure to 30 IU/mL hyaluronidase. After washing,
GV oocytes and M II eggs without surrounding cumulus
cells were treated as follows.
Experimental Treatments of Oocytes and Eggs
Stock solutions of PMA, 4 a-phorbol 12,13-didecano-
ate (4 a-PDD), calphostin C (Calbiochem, La Jolla, CA),
staurosporine, and okadaic acid (OA) were prepared
with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at 1.62 mM, 1.62 mM,
500 µM, 2 mM and 0.2 mM, respectively, and were
stored frozen at –20°C. Prior to use, they were diluted
with T6 medium.
To detect MAP kinase changes during normal matura-
tion and fertilization in vitro, GV oocytes were cultured
in T6 medium containing 4 mg/mL BSA, and different
groups of samples were collected for immunoblotting,
i.e., GV oocytes from premature mice, GV oocytes from
adult mice, GV oocytes collected 1.5 hr after incubation,
GVBD oocytes collected 1.5 hr after incubation, oocytes
collected 2 hr post-GVBD, oocytes still arresting at the
GV stage 4 hr after incubation, oocytes incubated for 7
hr, 11 hr (without GV), and 15 hr (mature oocytes with
PB1). Metaphase II eggs were inseminated in T6 me-
dium containing 20 mg/mL BSA with sperm that had
been previously capacitated for 1.5 hr. Pronuclear
formation was evaluated and samples for immunoblot-
ting were collected at different times after insemina-
tion. The culture conditions were 36.5°C, 5% CO2 in air,
and 100% humidity.
To study the role of PMA on cell cycle regulation and
MAP kinase changes, GV oocytes were cultured for 4 hr
in T6 medium containing 4 mg/mL BSA and different
concentrations of PMA. Part of the oocytes were ﬁrst
treated with 162 nM PMA for 10 min and then cultured
in PMA-free medium for up to 4 hr, after washing three
times. The M II eggs collected 15 hr or 19 hr after hCG
administration were treated with different concentra-
tions of PMA for up to 8 hr in T6 medium containing 20
mg/mL BSA. Part of the eggs were ﬁrst treated with 162
nM PMA for 5 or 10 min and then cultured in PMA-free
medium for up to 8 hr. Oocytes and eggs cultured in
drug-free medium and those treated with 162 nM 4
a-PDD were adopted as the controls. The GVBD was
evaluated under a stereomicroscope, and pronuclear
formation was evaluated under an inverted phase-
contrast microscope, and samples for immunoblotting
were collected after different treatments or at different
times after the same treatment.
To clarify whether short-time treatment of oocytes
and eggs with PMA results in MAP kinase activation as
in other cell lines, GV oocytes and M II eggs were
treated with 162 nM PMA for up to 1 hr. Samples for
immunoblotting were collected 5 min, 15 min, 30 min,
and 1 hr after incubation.
To investigate the possibility that OA-sensitive pro-
tein phosphatases act in the regulation of the meiotic
cell cycle and MAP kinase activity changes caused by
PMA, GV oocytes and M II eggs were cultured in the
medium containing both 162 nM PMA and 2 µM OA.
The GVBD and pronuclear formation were judged, and
samples for immunoblotting were collected 4 and 8 hr
after treatment, respectively.
To further investigate possible involvement of PKC in
PMA-induced GVBD inhibition and pronuclear forma-
tion, oocytes and eggs were ﬁrst treated with 500 nM
speciﬁc PKC inhibitor, calphostin C, for 8 min under
light which was provided by a 40-W ﬂuorescent lamp
MAP KINASE REGULATION BY PMA IN OOCYTES AND EGGS
311

MAP KINASE REGULATION BY PMA IN OOCYTES AND EGGS
(15 cm from the oocytes or eggs), or with 30 nM PKC
inhibitor, staurosporine, for 15 min, and then cultured
in the medium containing both 162 nM PMA and one of
the PKC inhibitors. After evaluation of GVBD 4 and 16
hr after treatment, and of pronuclear formation 8 hr
after treatment, samples were collected for immunoblot-
All of the above experiments were repeated at least
Electrophoresis and Immunoblotting
Oocytes and eggs were collected in the SDS sample
buffer and heated to 100°C for 4 min. After cooling on
ice for 4 min and centrifuging at 14,000 rpm for 5 min,
samples were frozen at 270°C until use. The proteins
were separated by SDS-PAGE with a 4% stacking gel
and a 10% separating gel for 50 min at 188 V and were
electrophoretically transferred onto PROTRAN nitrocel-
lulose membrane (Schleicher & Schuell, Germany) for 1
hr, 200 mA, at 4°C. After blocking for 30 min in 5% BSA
in 20 mM Tris, 137 mM NaCl (TBS) (pH 7.6), which
contains 0.1% Tween-20 (TBS-T), the membrane was
incubated overnight at 4°C with polyclonal anti-
ActiveTM MAPK antibody (Promega, Madison, WI, lot
no. 64840) diluted 1:5,000 in TBS. This antibody selec-
tively recognizes phosphorylated (active) forms of ERK1
and ERK2 (Schaefer and Moravec, 1996). After two
washes of 15 min each in TBS-T, the membrane was
incubated for 1 hr at room temperature with donkey-
anti-rabbit IgG (Jackson ImmunoResearch Laborato-
ries, Inc.) diluted 1:6,000 in TBS-T. The membrane was
washed twice in TBS-T and then processed using the
ECL detection system (Amersham). For all of the
treatments, 20 eggs were loaded into each lane.
For reprobing, the blots were stripped of bound
antibodies by washing in a stripping buffer (100 mM
b-mercaptoethanol, 20% SDS, 62.5 mM Tris, pH 6.7) at
50°C for 30 min. The membrane was reprobed with
polyclonal anti-ERK2 antibody (Santa Cruz Biotechnol-
ogy, Inc., Santa Cruz, CA, lot no. 1276) diluted 1:3,000,
using the same procedure described above. All of the
experiments were repeated at least twice.
All data on oocyte maturation and egg activation
were evaluated by x2 analysis. Eggs that had degener-
ated, or those with degenerating signs as indicated by
darkening cytoplasm, were not included. The relative
MAP kinase activity after each treatment was deter-
mined by densitometric scan of the band. Student’s
t-test. Differences at P , 0.05 were considered signiﬁ-
Cell Cycle Progression and MAP Kinase Changes
During Meiotic Maturation and Fertilization
To detect MAP kinase changes during mouse oocyte
maturation and egg fertilization, the separated pro-
teins were ﬁrst probed with anti-ActiveTM MAPK anti-
body to show MAP kinase activity, and the same
nitrocellulose membrane was then reprobed with anti-
ERK2 antibody after stripping to show the existence of
this kinase. MAP kinase was already expressed in GV
oocytes obtained from premature mice. After incubat-
ing for 1.5 hr, 62.9% (100/159) of the oocytes from
sexually mature mice remained at the GV stage, and
the others underwent GVBD, in which MAP kinase
activation was not yet detected (Fig. 1). Mitogen-
activated protein kinase was activated 2 hr after GVBD,
and its activity remained high for 7 hr, 11 hr, and 15 hr
after incubation. After 4 hr of incubation, 87.4% (174/
199) of the oocytes underwent GVBD, while the rest,
12.6% (25/199), were still arrested at the GV stage. No
MAP kinase activation was detected in the latter group
(Fig. 1). Fertilization of M II eggs revealed a signiﬁcant
activation of MAP kinase activity 2.5 hr after insemina-
tion (Fig. 2). Its activity was decreased to the control
level 4 hr after insemination when a few eggs (7.1%,
6/84) formed pronuclei, and it was almost completely
inactivated 8 hr after insemination when most eggs
(81.3%, 65/80) formed pronuclei (Fig. 2). Neither synthe-
sis/degradation nor electrophoretic mobility shift of this
kinase was observed after the kinase was activated or
inactivated during oocyte maturation or egg fertiliza-
The Effect of PMA on GVBD and MAP Kinase
As shown in Table 1, the addition of the phorbol ester,
PMA, to the incubation medium inhibited spontaneous
resumption of meiotic maturation (GVBD) in a dose-
dependent manner. The effective concentration of PMA
Fig. 1. Changes in MAP kinase during mouse oocytes maturation
in vitro. Oocytes at the GV stage were collected and cultured in vitro.
Samples for immunoblotting were collected at different times after
incubation and evaluation of GVBD. Extracts were separated by 10%
SDS-PAGE and transferred onto nitrocellulose membrane. A total of
20 oocytes was loaded into each lane. Blots were incubated with a
polyclonal anti-ActiveTM MAP kinase antibody to show MAP kinase
activity (top). The same blots were subsequently stripped and re-
probed with polyclonal anti-ERK2 antibody to show the existence of
this kinase (bottom). The positions of ERK1 and ERK2 are indicated
on the right. The same treatments were applied in all subsequent
ﬁgures. Lane 1: GV oocytes from premature mice; lane 2: GV oocytes
from adult mice; lane 3: GV oocytes from adult mice collected 1.5 hr
after incubation; lane 4: GVBD oocytes collected 1.5 hr after incuba-
tion; lane 5: oocytes collected at 2 hr post-GVBD; lane 6: oocytes still
arresting at GV stage 4 hr after incubation; lane 7: oocytes incubated
for 7 hr (without GV); lane 8: oocytes incubated for 11 hr (without GV);
lane 9: oocytes incubated for 15 hr (mature oocytes with PB1). There
were no changes in the MAP kinase amount during oocyte maturation.
This blot is representative of three similar experiments.
to inhibit GVBD was 1.62 nM (62.5% inhibition), with
almost maximal inhibition (88.7%) at concentrations of
16.2 nM. At 162 nM PMA, transient treatment for 10
min or chronic treatment caused almost the same
inhibition when observed 4 hr after incubation. Inhibi-
tion of GVBD by PMA was well correlated with inhibi-
tion of MAP kinase activation (Fig. 3). The PMA began
to inhibit MAP kinase activation at 1.62 nM and
exerted its maximal pharmacological effect over 16.2
nM. Chronic treatment or 10-min transient treatment
of oocytes with PMA similarly inhibited MAP kinase
activation. Biologically inactive 4 a-PDD did not affect
MAP kinase activation and was found to have effects on
neither GVBD nor MAP kinase activity (Table 1 and
The Effects of PMA on Metaphase-Interphase
Transition and MAP Kinase Activity in M II Eggs
The PMA induced interphase transition in eggs col-
lected 19 hr after hCG administration in a dose-
dependent manner, with 16.2 nM being the minimum
effective dosage, which caused 36% of the eggs to form
pronuclei (Table 2). Treatment of eggs with 162 nM
PMA revealed a time-dependent pronuclear formation,
with a nearly maximal effect shown after 10 min of
incubation (Table 3). The PMA began to inactivate MAP
kinase at 16.2 nM. Chronic treatment or 10-min tran-
sient treatment of eggs with PMA similarly decreased
MAP kinase activity 8 hr after treatment (Fig. 4),
results which are well correlated with pronuclear forma-
tion (Table 3). When eggs were treated with 162 nM
PMA for different times, MAP kinase activity decreased
4 hr after treatment and further decreased at 8 hr (Fig.
5), which was also well correlated with PN formation
(Table 3). The PMA did not cause the emission of the
second polar body when observed at different times of
incubation. The activation of mouse eggs by PMA was
dependent on the eggs’ age. When eggs collected 15 hr
after hCG administration were treated with PMA, only

When oocytes or eggs were incubated with 162 nM
PMA for a short term, neither cell cycle progression nor
MAP kinase activity changes were observed. There was
no increase in MAP kinase activity after 5 min, 15 min,

PKC Inhibitor, Calphostin C, and Protein
Phosphatase Inhibitor, OA, Abolish the Effect
of PMA on Meiotic Cell Cycle Progression
When eggs and oocytes were pretreated with a spe-
ciﬁc PKC inhibitor, calphostin C, and then incubated in
medium containing both PMA and this inhibitor, both
pronuclear formation and GVBD inhibition were signiﬁ-
cantly decreased 8 hr and 16 hr after treatment,
respectively (P , 0.01) (Fig. 7 and Table 1). Another
PKC inhibitor, staurosporine, also signiﬁcantly de-
creased the proportion of oocyte and eggs at interphase
induced by PMA, but it was not as effective as calphos-
tin C (Table 1 and Fig. 7). Correspondingly, the decrease
in MAP kinase activity induced by PMA was reversed to
a certain extent by PKC inhibitors, calphostin C and
staurosporine, in oocytes and eggs (Fig. 8). The propor-
tion of oocytes or eggs in interphase was well consistent
with the degree of MAP kinase inactivation in each case
(Table 1 and Figs. 7, 8). Protein phosphatase inhibitor,
OA, also overcame the GVBD inhibition in oocytes,
metaphase-interphase transition in eggs, and MAP
kinase downregulation induced by PMA (Table 1 and

cross-talks between this kinase and other signal mol-
Our results showed that MAP kinase was still in a
dephosphorylated (inactivated) form in oocytes that
just ﬁnished GVBD at 1.5 hr postincubation in vitro,
although the protein was synthesized even in GV
oocytes obtained from premature mice. The MAP ki-
nase was activated (phosphorylated) 2 hr after GVBD
(4 hr after incubation). However, activation did not
occur in the oocytes that failed to go through GVBD
after culturing in vitro for 4 hr. We suggest that the
ability of oocyte to phosphorylate MAP kinase is re-
quired for meiosis resumption during normal oocyte
maturation. However, it is not the activation of MAP
kinase itself, but the activation of some upstream
molecule(s) that regulates MAP kinase activity that is
required for the resumption of the cell cycle. When the
oocyte were treated with PKC activator, PMA, both
GVBD inhibition and MAP kinase activity downregula-
tion occurred in a dose-dependent manner. It is logical
to conclude that PMA inhibits GVBD via action upon
the upstream molecules that are required for MAP
kinase phosphorylation. One such molecule could be
MPF, since the increase in MPF activity proceeds the
increase in MAP kinase activity during oocyte matura-
tion (Verlhac et al., 1994), and OA, which stimulates
GVBD and MAP kinase activation as revealed in this
study, also activates MPF (Rime and Ozon, 1990).
However, other molecules may not be excluded. Chesnel
and Eppig (1995) reported that stimulation of preco-
cious GVBD and subsequent MAP kinase activation by
OA occurred without p34cdc2 kinase activation in
GVBD-incompetent mouse oocytes.
We also found that MAP kinase was still in a phos-
phorylated form at 4 hr postinsemination, but it was
dephosphorylated at 8 hr, when most eggs formed
pronuclei. When treated with different concentrations
of PMA, or treated with a certain concentration of PMA

MAP KINASE REGULATION BY PMA IN OOCYTES AND EGGS
for different times, the proportion of eggs that formed
pronuclei was consistent with the degree of MAP kinase
dephosphorylation. On the other hand, treatment of
eggs with protein phosphatase inhibitor, OA, abolished
the interphase entry and MAP kinase inactivation
caused by PMA. Therefore, we suggest that PMA may
ﬁrst act upon upstream molecules of the cascade and
ﬁnally inactivate MAP kinase, leading to interphase
transition. The results also suggest that protein phos-
phatases may be involved in this cascade. Since we
found that a lower concentration of PMA was needed to
inhibit GVBD in oocytes than to induce the transition to
interphase in M II eggs, we suggest that a stronger
stimulus was needed to dephosphorylate active MAP
kinase in eggs than to keep the inactive form of MAP
kinase from being phosphorylated in mouse oocytes.
Previous studies on the role of PKC in oocyte matura-
tion and egg activation have been conducted by using
PKC activators and inhibitors, and different conclu-
sions have been reached by several authors in studies
on various animals. For example, PKC activators were
shown to stimulate GVBD in rat and bovine oocytes
(Aberdam and Dekel, 1985; Rose-Hellekant and Bavis-
ter, 1996), but opposite effects were observed in mouse
oocytes (Urner and Schorderet-Slatkine, 1984; Bornslae-
ger et al., 1986; LeFevre et al., 1992). Most authors
found that phorbol esters induced metaphase-inter-
phase transition in mouse and hamster eggs (Colonna
et al., 1989; Gallicano et al., 1993; Moses and Kline,
1995), but some authors did not ﬁnd this effect in
mouse, hamster, rat, or pig eggs (Endo et al., 1987;
Ducibella et al., 1993; Moore et al., 1995; Sun et al.,
1997; Raz et al., 1998). In the present experiment, we
found that activation of mouse eggs by PMA depends on
the egg age, and older eggs are easier to activate.
Treatment of oocytes or eggs with PMA for 10 min or
chronic treatment revealed a similar inhibition of GVBD
or stimulation of metaphase-interphase transition when
observed 4 and 8 hr after incubation, respectively. Since
PMA can either upregulate or downregulate PKC activ-
ity, depending on the concentration used and the expo-
sure time, and since chronic treatment with a high
concentration of PMA downregulates PKC activity,
interpretation of its effects should be made with caution
(Couldwell et al., 1994). In our experiment, the concen-
trations of PKC activator, PMA, and PKC inhibitors,
calphostin C and staurosporine, were based on previ-
ously published reports (Kobayashi et al., 1989; Galli-
cano et al., 1993; Moses and Kline, 1995). Our results
showed that treatment of oocytes and eggs with low
concentrations of PMA affected the cell cycle progres-
sion within a relatively short time. For example, 1.62
nM PMA effectively inhibited GVBD 2 hr after incuba-
tion. Chronic treatment of K562 cells with such a
dosage for 24 hr has been shown to upregulate PKC
activity (Racke et al., 1997). By contrast, the biologi-
cally inactive form of phorbol ester, 4a-PDD, had effects
on neither GVBD nor metaphase-interphase transition.
Additionally, speciﬁc PKC inhibitor, calphostin C, over-
came the effect of PMA on both events. Therefore, it is
possible that inhibition of GVBD in oocytes and stimu-
lation of metaphase-interphase transition in eggs by
PMA are mediated via activation of PKC. However,
whether PKC is involved in physiological process of
oocyte maturation and fertilization is still controver-
sial. Ducibella and LeFevre (1997) reported that PKC
antagonists, bisindolylmaleimide and chelerytherine,
which block PMA-induced effects, have essentially no
effect on sperm-induced egg activation. This result
suggests that PKC activation plays little, if any, role in
the normal course of events during fertilization. On the
other hand, Gallicano et al (1997) recently proved for
the ﬁrst time, at a biochemical level, that activation of
PKC after fertilization is required for remodeling the
mouse egg into the zygote. Other evidence that sup-
ports the physiological involvement of PKC in normal
egg activation is that light-activated PKC inhibitor,
calphostin C, can inhibit metaphase-interphase transi-
tion induced by sperm (Francione et al., 1997). Since
eggs collected 15 hr post-hCG were not as responsive to

PMA stimulation as those collected 19 hr post-hCG, we
suggest that PKC activation does act upstream of the
MAP kinase inactivation but that other factors are also
involved. Maturation-promoting factor is a key protein
kinase that regulates meiotic cell cycle progression
during egg activation. However, it has been shown that
PMA did not result in a reduction in histone H1 kinase
activity, although it induced the formation of inter-
phase pronucleus-like structures in mouse eggs (Moore
et al., 1995). Postovulatory eggs undergo time-depen-
dent cytoplasmic changes (Xu et al., 1997), which may
make the cross-talk among pathways stringently regu-
lated in the eggs collected 19 hr post-hCG, and thus
Unlike the situation in other cell lines (Reiss et al.,
1997), we did not ﬁnd a prompt increase in MAP kinase
after treatment of mouse oocytes or eggs by PMA. In
contrast, we found an inhibition of MAP kinase activa-
tion in oocytes and a decrease in MAP kinase activity in
eggs when PMA was added to the culture. The inactive
form of phorbol ester, 4a-PDD, had little effect on MAP
kinase activity. Additionally, when eggs were pre-
treated with PKC inhibitors, calphostin C or staurospo-
rine, MAP kinase downregulation by PMA was signiﬁ-
cantly alleviated. Thus, we suggest that PMA activates
PKC, which then entrains a cascade of events and
ultimately downregulates MAP kinase in immature
mouse oocytes and mature M II eggs. However, we
should remember that no one has ever directly proved
on a biochemical level that PKC activators indeed
modulate PKC activity in mouse oocytes or eggs (Duci-
bella and LeFevre, 1997). Additionally, different PKC
isoenzymes are regulated differently by PKC modula-
tors, and they may interact with different proteins, may
phosphorylate different substrates, and may even have
opposing roles in cellular processes (Messina et al.,
1992; Assert et al., 1996; Hofmann, 1997). There is still
a long way to go to make clear the role of PKC isoforms
in meiotic regulation and the relationship between the
MAP kinase cascade and different PKC isoforms, owing
to present technical limitations. The present study lays
the groundwork for future studies on these problems.
In conclusion, our data suggest that PKC activator,
PMA, entrains a cascade of events that ultimately
inhibit the MAP kinase activation and GVBD in mouse
oocytes and stimulates the MAP kinase inactivation
and metaphase-interphase transition in mouse eggs.
This effect can be abolished by protein phosphatase
inhibitor, okadaic acid, and speciﬁc PKC inhibitor,
calphostin C, suggesting the possible involvement of
these two enzymes in the events of the cascade.
